text: All right, good morning everyone and welcome to the second session of AI foundations.
Today's going to be the first session where we're actually building models and we're going to start with the simplest machine learning model, which is linear regression.
I'll give it over to Noit so we can get started.
Yeah, hello everyone.

So, just like we said in the previous session, we are going to begin with linear regression, which is usually the first algorithm that is taught in almost any machine learning 101 class—even in deep learning this is taught at the beginning.

The reason for it is because the basic learning principles that linear regression uses are almost the same across a wide variety of algorithms—particularly in deep learning it is throughout the same, and for some of the traditional learning algorithms the procedure is also the same.
That's why this is something that is taught at the beginning.
So let me share my screen and I'll try to build the intuition behind what is linear regression and why do we do it.
How many people here are familiar with linear regression already?
I took a college course on it, but I definitely didn't pay attention to that part of it.
I think I learned it in statistics, but that's pretty much it.
Yeah, so this is usually borrowed from statistics.
Linear regression existed a long time before someone even used the term machine learning.

So this is something that statisticians usually make memes about—machine learning was statistics, and then they decorated it and stopped calling it machine learning, when fundamentally it is statistics at play.
That's something that people usually say about machine learning anyway.

So, linear regression—if you remember the nomenclature that we discussed, the taxonomy of machine learning, this falls under supervised learning, and particularly linear regression deals with regression problems.
Regression is a type of problem where the goal is to predict a continuous variable.
Values are continuous and don't come from any predefined classes.

The basic idea is that if you have some historical data, let's call it X and Y, where X contains the features which are impacting the target or which are going to contribute towards the target, which is why these are the features called the label.
Our goal is to come up with a function which approximates or maps this input to output.
If we can somehow model that relationship between input and output, we can then make use of that function to make predictions for unseen data.
Suppose we'll take a hypothetical problem of house price prediction.
Suppose X is the area of the house and Y is the corresponding price of the house.
For now we're just taking one feature, but this principle can be applied or the equation stays the same for any number of features, but just for simplicity let's take just the area of the house.
So we have some historical data: we have areas for it, let's just put some random values there—10, prices let's say 50, 20, and 100, and whatever.
Now the goal is to make use of this data in order to learn the relationship or to learn the mapping between this input and output.
Now, how can we do that?
There are a wide variety of ways that we can do this; linear regression is just one way of doing it.
So if I try to plot this hypothetical data, what I could see would be somewhat like this: I'm trying to plot a scatter plot of it.
So on the x-axis we have the area, which is X, and on the y-axis we have the price.
So if I have a bunch of such historical data, we can just simply put an area in the corresponding price and we could get something like this.
So with linear regression, the basic idea is we need to model this relationship using a function, and the function that linear regression uses is a function of a straight line.
The equation of linear regression says something like this: Y hat, which is the output, equals WX + B, and I'm sure you people recognize this equation—this is the equation of a straight line.
What is w here? Can anyone tell me the slope is the slope and B is the Y intercept?
So if I take an arbitrary line and just put it in here, this is just one way of modeling this data using a straight line, but of course there are numerous ways that we can draw this straight line.
This is also a fit to this data, this could also be a fit to this data, and so on.
There are numerous ways—the question is which one is the best fit line to this data and how do we come up with such a line?
That's the whole job of training a linear regression: in order to come up with a line which best fits this data.
Once we come up with a line which best fits this data, we can then simply make use of that equation of the line in order to predict the price for any house.
So this x here is the area of the house—we just simply have to put the area, and then using this W and B, it would tell us the price of the house.
Initially, this W and B we don't know what should be the slope of the line, what should be the Y intercept—that's why these are randomly initialized.
We just simply put some values for them, but because we put random values for them, the obvious thing is that the line that we are going to have would not be the best fit to this data.
So then the question is how do we refine this line such that it becomes the best fit to this data?
In order to do that, we introduced some other terminologies—one of it is called the cost function.
So the job of the cost function is whatever the line predicts and whatever the true output is, which comes from the data itself, compute the difference between those two, which is what your cost is.
Now initially, because these are randomly initialized, the cost is going to be very high, and our job is to somehow reduce this cost.

The value for this cost, and if we manage to reduce the cost, in other words we would be trying to make this y hat as close as possible to Y because in that way the difference between them would be reduced.
Now for different tasks, different loss functions are used.
We are currently dealing with regression, so for regression the typical
The loss function that is used is called MSE (mean squared error), and I'm very sure you all know this, so I'll just put the equation for it.

Mean squared error simply says: take the prediction of the line, take the true output, subtract it from it, square the difference, sum it over all the examples that you have, and then divide it by the total number of examples.
This here is the total number of examples that you have in your historical data.
Do you have anything to ask till this point?
Yeah, the cost function that you mention here—is that equal to the loss function? Like, is that the same, just a different word?
Yeah, so usually they're used interchangeably, but I've heard it from one of the tutors somewhere that cost is usually the term given to the entire sum that you get at the end.
So this is cost, while loss is computed for a single example.

If I make a prediction for a single example—let's say this one—and I get some prediction from it and I compute the difference between the prediction and the true output, that is the loss for this example.
But when you accumulate it across the entire data, then it is called the cost function.
But this distinction is not very solid, so people usually use these interchangeably.
Okay, okay. Another quick question: in this case, the cost or the loss could be either on the x-axis or on the y-axis, right?
So are there two values that you get as a cost, or is it just one value?
No, it's just one value.
So let's say we take a line—let's say this is our initial value for w and b that we randomly initialize—it got us this line.
What is the cost here? Well, the cost is: for this line, for instance, we take this particular example here, and this is the corresponding value.
What we do is we take that value, we plug it inside the equation of this line.
This line is going to produce something whose value would be this, right?
And the true output, or the true value for that, is this.
Now, what is the cost here? Cost is this difference here that you see.
You take that difference, you square it.
It is the difference and you compute it not just for this one, you also compute it for this one, for this one, for that one, and so on for the entire data.
And then you accumulate all of them together and then divide it by the total number of examples, which is what this mean represents in this MSE.
Mean is because you are averaging; squared is because you are squaring the difference, and error is the error that is the difference between the two.
Now if we take a close look at this cost function, what do we have here inside this cost function?
We have Y, which comes from the data itself, and then we have y hat, which is the prediction from the model.
If I try to plug in that equation here, what I would see is WX + b - y hat squared.
The change, the layout—that's better, can you see?
Now yeah, that's good, thank you.
Okay, so just like I said initially, the value for this cost is very high, and the goal is to reduce this cost.
Now, what can we do in order to reduce this?
Can we change Y here in order to reduce the value?
No, because Y is determined by the data—Y is the actual value.
Right, exactly; Y comes from the data, we cannot change that.
What can we change?
Can we change this n in order to reduce the value?
No, and it's given by the number of data points.
X also comes from data; we cannot change that as well.
What are we left with?
We are left with these two: W and B.
We can only change these two in order to reduce the value for this entire function.
And that's what training a model is—these are called the parameters.

We need to set the values for these parameters such that whatever output this model is producing becomes as close as possible to the true output, so that the differences are minimized and the entire cost function is minimized.
That's the whole goal.
And if someone is from a mathematics background, they would recognize this—it's a very well-known problem in mathematics called optimization.
So optimization in mathematics just simply means maximizing or minimizing the value of a function based on some
The parameters here are the function whose value we need to minimize, the cost function, usually represented by J or C, whatever doesn't matter.
The parameters that we have are W and B.
This cost function is parameterized by W and B.
We need to change the values for W and B such that the value of this cost is reduced.

Now, since I said this is a very well-known problem in mathematics, which means there are well-suited tools in mathematics already out there which we can make use of in order to reduce the value of this cost function.
Can anyone tell me any method?
Well, maybe we could use gradient descent perhaps?
Yeah, so gradient descent is a very popular optimization algorithm that is used in almost all the machine learning optimization problems.
What is the idea behind gradient descent?
Let me write the equation for gradient descent and then we'll talk about its conceptual background.
The equation of gradient descent says that you need to update the values for W and B in this way in order to reduce the value for the cost function.
This is for W.
And this is for B.
There are a few terms that we need to talk about here.
Let's first take this term.
In the previous session, I told you to take a look into derivatives.
Here's where they are used.
What we are simply doing here is that we are taking the derivative of the cost function with respect to W.
Now, can anyone tell me what a derivative does?
So J is the cost function, right?
Is that just a normal symbol used for cost?
Yeah, this is.
I've seen it in a few places.
You can use C if this confuses you, but that doesn't really matter.
I wanted to clarify that.
Yeah, but what is the derivative?
So the derivative here would be: if we change W by a tiny amount, how much does that affect the larger cost function J?
Yeah, we'll talk about that.
But the concept of derivative is that it computes the instantaneous change in one quantity or one function with respect to another quantity.
So here:
The first function that we want to compute the change in with respect to the other one—the first one is the cost and another one is the W.
In other words, we want to see what happens to the cost if we change this W.
Now, why do we want to compute that?
Well, because the goal is to reduce the value for the cost function, and if you want to reduce the value for something, you need to first know how the value of it changes with respect to that.
Because if you don't know how the value changes, you cannot change, you cannot reduce the value of it.
I gave this example in some previous session: there's a water tank and there's a knob, and someone asks you to turn the knob in such a way so that the flow of water is reduced.
Now, before you are able to turn the knob in such a way to reduce the flow of water, you would first need to know what happens if I turn the knob towards the left or towards the right.
If you figure out that turning it towards the left increases the flow of water, you automatically know that I need to turn it towards the right in order to reduce the flow of water.
This is exactly the same thing at play here.
We first want to know how the cost changes with respect to changing W.
If we can figure out that, we can then make use of that information in order to change W in such a way which would reduce the overall value of the cost function.
Let's see how this turns out graphically.
So I'll just simply plot these equations—plot these plots, although these are arbitrary, but if you try to repeat this experiment with a computer, you'd see the exact same phenomena.
So let's take this W on the x-axis and the cost on the y-axis.
So I'm just simply plotting the cost against the value of W.
In other words, I'm taking this function here and plugging in some values for W and plotting the resultant values for J.
You'd ask me, we don't know B here—let's just forget about B for a second.
We are just simply computing, fixing the value for B and just computing the cost against W.
The natural, obvious thing is that for some values of W, the cost is going to be very high, and for other values of W, the cost is going to be very low.
And if you plot this across a wide range of W values, you would see something like
This simply tells you that for some values of w, the cost is low.
For instance, here for other values of w, for instance here, cost is high or here.
And so on. Let's assume that the cost function is some arbitrary curve.
Remember, we randomly initialize the values for w and b.
Let's say the random initialization of w got us here—we arbitrarily put a value of w which was here.
If we compute the value for the cost function at that w, the corresponding cost is here.
Now let's see what happens when we compute the derivative of this cost with respect to w.
If you would have studied the concept of derivatives, a derivative is nothing but the slope of a tangent line drawn to the curve at that exact location.
You people know this already?
No one knows about it?
Yeah, I think I saw people nodding their heads, but I don't see your heads when I'm sharing my screen, so that's why I'm just blank.
Okay, so what did you say, James? No one knows about it?
No, I think I see people nodding their heads in agreement with the concept.
Yeah, I remember it from algebra.
Okay, so if we start talking about derivatives, I think the rest of the discussion would be consumed by that.
That's why I suggested to watch something on the concept of derivatives.
So I'll just briefly touch upon it, but if something confuses you, you can ask me questions as well.
Maybe that would leave us some time, but if you would prefer otherwise, you can then later on study the concept of derivatives and this whole story that I'm telling here would make much more sense.
But what a derivative simply does is it is like the slope of a tangent line.
What do I mean by that?
Let's just take an example.
Let's say we have a function, or let's say a car starts from some point and it travels and covers some distance.
So let's say the distance is on the y-axis and the time is on the x-axis.
So maybe the distance that it

Covers. I don't know if it is making much sense, but let's say for some durations of time the car is traveling at one speed and for other durations of time the car is traveling at a different speed, and that's why the distance it is covering at different instances of time is different.

Now, if someone asks you what is the speed of this car, you can simply take the total distance and divide it by the total time, so total distance divided by total time, and this would give you the speed of the car.

In other words, it would tell you a change in distance with respect to time, but this is called the average speed because we clearly see that when the car starts it is slow, it then gains speed, and then while reaching the destination it again slows down.
So at different instances of time the speed of the car is different. It's not average across the whole journey, and that's where derivatives come into play.

Derivatives are not computing the average rate of change; it is computing the instantaneous rate of change, or the instantaneous change in one quantity with respect to another quantity, and instantaneous change is what we are very much interested in.

Suppose if I am interested in figuring out the instantaneous change, well, what I can do is instead of taking the total distance covered and dividing by the total time taken, I can zoom into a particular region where I would shrink the time so much that the times become very small.

So I take, let's say, this time. Let's call it T1, and I add a very small quantity of time to it, a very tiny amount. At this time, the distance that it was covering was the change in distance. I'm sorry—the distance covered is this much, and at that very tiny addition of time, the distance it covered slightly increased.
Now, how can we compute the speed or the rate of change of distance with respect to time for this particular region?

What I can simply do is take the distance. Let's call it S, and the change in distance. Let's call that delta S, and I can simply do something like this: S plus delta S minus S divided by T1 plus delta T minus T1. In other words, I'm just taking that small change in distance and dividing it
By the small change in time and that would give me the rate of change of distance with respect to time for that particular region.
It's not currently taking the entire journey; it's just focusing or zooming in on that particular region.
In other words, this is derivative—this is the whole concept of derivative.
You just have to introduce limits here, so you say limit delta T approaches zero, and then suddenly this definition is called derivatives in calculus.
Now, if you haven't seen this anywhere before, I don't assume you to learn this instantly just by the small description that I've given you.
But if you have seen this before, then I expect you would recall these concepts.
We are just introducing tiny changes into the time, which corresponds to tiny changes in the distance, and then we are computing or dividing the distance by time.
Now I'm explaining this in terms of distance and time, but you can apply the same concept across any two quantities.
You take a first quantity whose rate of change you want to calculate, and then you simply introduce a very tiny change in it.

A very tiny change is what we are saying, but in the language of mathematics we use limits for that, so we say that limit delta T approaches zero, meaning the change is so small that it is almost zero, but it's not zero.
In other words, if you then graphically see this thing, the definition of slope is the rise over run, simply meaning change in y divided by change in x—this is slope.
Do you know what a slope is?
Yeah, yes, okay.
So why do we say that our derivative is nothing but the slope of the tangent line?

Well, because if you see here, this is exactly what we are doing: we are taking a slightly introduced change in y, which here is distance by the way, and dividing it by a slightly introduced change in x, which is here time.
And that's why it is said that derivatives is nothing but the slope of the tangent drawn to that particular instant that we are looking into.
I hope it is making sense.
Some sense to you can I move on from this towards our original discussion, or do you have anything to ask here?
I have a quick question. I don't know if this makes any sense, but isn't the section—the size of the section that you're taking—would that relate to the resolution of the data?
I'm just thinking, if there is some measurement error or something like that, if you take a section that you're picking that's too small, then that could result in a wrong result.
Which section are you talking about here?
You mentioned that you have this tiny section that you're taking, and in this example, for the distance traveled, you're zooming in on this tiny part, this tiny change.
I'm just wondering if that's something that you need to set according to the resolution of the data.

What I understand from your question is that when I said the change is very small—of course, if you are talking about derivatives, they assume this change to be when you apply limits, it says the change is so small that it is almost zero.
But we know that if you are talking about computers, computers wouldn't know how small we are talking about—you still have to fix some value for it.
So when it is computing the derivative, it would still take this change to be some real number.
Of course, if you make that resolution—if the resolution is not that much—you are introducing some error into it.

But if you then increase the resolution, meaning if you make the change very small—suppose initially you set the change to be 0.01 because that's all the memory that you want to assign to it—now if you increase it, you make it 0.00 and let's say a ten number of zeros and then one.
The memory it's going to take would be larger than what the first one was taking, because now you would need more storage in order to store such a number.
So it depends on that, but this is a discussion that is way off from what we are discussing.
It has nothing to do with our data here.

I'm not sure if you are asking this in that context or not, but here what we are simply doing in this plot here is we are having some values for w and we are computing the corresponding cost according to this equation here.
The goal is now to reduce the value for this cost function.
How can we do that now?
Whatever I'm explaining here, this is something that existed for centuries.
This is not something that machine learning introduced.
Optimization is something that was widely used across many different areas.
Gradient descent is one way of optimizing the values of equations.
The basic concept is you take the function and you compute its derivative.
Once you have the derivative, the derivative is nothing but the slope of a tangent line drawn to that particular point that you are interested in.
Here the slope would look somewhat like this.
We know that lines that appear like this have a slope of zero because it's a perfectly horizontal line.
Lines that appear like this have a slope of infinity because it's a perfectly vertical line.
Anything that falls on this side is having a positive slope.
Anything that falls on this side is having a negative slope.
This is known to everyone.
Okay, so let's see how this slope and this discussion plays out here in the gradient descent.
We see that the slope of this line is positive.
We are not interested in looking into exact values right now.
This is just conceptual understanding, so we would just use some positive value.
Now let's see what happens.
This is the current value of w.
This is going to give us some positive value, which we saw from here.
What happens to this updated value for w?
We just simply take the previous w and subtract from it.
Let's just fix this to be one.
I'll talk about this alpha term, what it is, but let's just say it is one for now.
One times this some positive value.

So this is nine minus three, and the new value for it is six.

It would appear somewhere below that.

Let's call it W.

Now I'll talk about this Alpha term and then you can ask me as many questions as you can, and I would love to remove your confusions from any part of this discussion that we had.
What is Alpha? This is called the learning rate.
Learning rate or Alpha controls how much change you would like to introduce into this W.

If you set the value for this Alpha to some high number, so maybe initially you were here, you computed the slope—it was some value—you multiply it with a very large value, and that's why the change that it introduced into the W would be huge.
So maybe from here, instead of coming here, it dropped you down to this point directly.

But again, if you do the computation again, you compute their derivative, multiplied with a very large number, instead of making you come here, this would maybe remove a very large chunk from it and it could shift you towards this side.
So instead of converging to this minimum point, it would diverge from that.
And again, if you update this, if you look at this left side here, the tangent, the slope of tangent would appear negative because it would be a falling line, somewhat like this.
The slope would be negative. If you take that negative value here, negative and negative becomes positive, and you are adding something to which would again push it towards the right.
So that's what happens on the negative side.
But the point that I'm trying to tell you here is that if Alpha is very large, instead of coming at this point, it would be diverging or fluctuating across this minimum value.
So that's why too large values for learning rates are not good.
But what happens if you set it too small?
If you set it too small, that's also not good because it would take longer for you to reach this minimum point because it will be taking baby steps to reach that point.

So at the initial iteration, it might update it to this point, and then this point, and this point, and something that could have been achieved after, let's say, 10 iterations, it could now take 30 iterations because of the very small learning rate.
So that's why this cannot be set too small, cannot be set too large.
There should be a sweet spot that you should put this value in order to balance the equation.
The question is: how
Do we know what should be the sweet spot?
We don't know that, this is why it's called a hyperparameter.
You experiment with this thing, so you try multiple learning rates.
Usually the learning rate that is tried by default in TensorFlow or in Scikit-learn is set at around 0.01.
But for some problems that would not work, so you'd have to change this maybe to 0.02 or 0.003, or maybe further reduce it to 0.001.
You can experiment with this thing, but for majority of the problems, 0.1 would work.
There are also other variations of how to set this learning rate we might talk about some other time, because I think I have already introduced lots of concepts here.

Yep, that's what's happening.

Can I ask a question regarding the learning rate?
Sure, I know you have mentioned that is an experimentation side, but I will be interested if what is your logic on how to find the sweet spot again?
I know you mentioned what will be the default.
That we can test, but what's your logic upon that?
You can expand more, okay?
So there's no mathematical way of coming up with a good learning rate, but the logic is:
If you try to implement this gradient descent equation, particularly looking at the values for the slope, usually the values you get are very large numbers.

Now if you remove the learning rate—let's say for a second you don't want to use this learning rate—the values for the slope are so large that if you start plugging them in directly, the original values for the W's would be very small compared to that.
That's why the changes you would be introducing would never bring you to the point of convergence; it would always diverge.
Sometimes it would blow up and give you a completely insane value.
That's why, in order to control those values, you have to put some number in there which would control the amount of change you are introducing.

Now when you see these values yourself—solving it and updating the values for W's—you see those values, and it automatically makes sense that you need to put some small value; you cannot put a large value.
The slopes are already getting you very large values.
Suppose there's a very sharp turn in the cost function.
If you look at this point, for instance, and compute the slope at that point, it would be a line that would be very close to a perfectly vertical line.
We know that the slope of a vertical line is infinity—it's a huge number.
So that's why the slopes are always getting you numbers that are very large compared to what the weight numbers are or what the values for those weights are.
That's why it makes logical sense that this slope value has to be something smaller; it has to be something that would control or reduce the original derivative value.
How do we come up with this exact value?
This is not something I would know myself if I'm trying to train a learning algorithm, and I would have no idea what value to put.
That's why literature studies become handy when you see other people training algorithms.
There's already a ton of knowledge that exists in the machine learning space.
You don't have to start from something completely blind and work your way towards this particular value.
That's why these values are put there on default.

But if they are not there on default, if you study some of the implementations, you see that people are using learning rates closer to these ones, and that's where you get an idea of what you should be doing.
The same discussions would be true for a lot of other hyperparameters.
This is just one hyperparameter that we have to tune ourselves.
The algorithm is not going to do that for us automatically.
What kind of features would we like to use?
Should we include square features, or if we talk about neural networks, should we introduce three layers or four layers?
Should we make the number of neurons decreasing as we progress towards the network, or should they be increasing?
All of these questions have no definite mathematical answers for them.
That's why you build upon the knowledge that already exists in literature.
You study people solving different problems using various techniques.

You just juggle or use experience with these things, spend a lot of time in order to come up with something that would work.

At initial iterations, the cost is very high, and as you train, as the iterations progress, the cost is reducing.
Let's say this is at the 90th iteration, and let's say this is at the 100th iteration.
At this point, you see that the loss is pretty much minimum and it's no longer decreasing—it's appearing flat after that point.

Not much significant reduction is happening after 100 iterations, so that's why I need to train it till 100 iterations.

I shouldn't be doing that, so you can then increase the number of iterations in order to further reduce the amount of cost.
This is one way, but there are other ways as well.

You can also put early stopping criteria—so you say you set an advanced criterion that if my cost is, let's say, below 0.001, I think it would do a reasonable job and I no longer need to train further.

I can stop there—this is called early stopping criteria.
